replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I like this idea mate - would you ground the sphere like in a tesla set up?

replies.author: @JuanPerez-gj8dl
replies.text:
Pardon me, but if the sphere is collecting an electrical charge like an antenna, then the same way for the charge to leave the antenna applies to the sphere.  Your battery can collect a charge from radio waves and since it is also acting as an antenna, it has a positive charge and it needs a ground for the electrical charge to flow to.  Just make sure the ground has the way for it to allow the extra charge to flow through it, so equilibrium is not obstructing it.  
Think of it in terms of a capacitor,  the top is positive and the bottom  plane is negative; and it passes right through the plane to the ground if it doesn't touch the negative plate on the way through.  The positive  and negative plates are in equilibrium, but if it has a path through to the ground it goes through it easier.  Lightning has to overcome the 
air barrier, but for a small voltage if you let it pass through it flows easier. 

Using a negative charge around the wire to allow the positive to pass through is a good way.  You might use a setup the way they do towers that don't have a good ground.  Using a radial system the definition is from:
“Book/Definitions”
Electrical Engineering Dictionary.
Ed. Phillip A. Laplante
Boca Raton: CRC Press LLC, 2000

Radial system -  a network of straight wires or other conductors radiating from the base of a vertical monopole antenna that simulates the presence of a highly conducting ground plane beneath the antenna. Typically, radial wires are approximately a quarter wavelength long and are arranged to have equiangular spacing between them. The radial wire ends at the base of the monopole are electrically bonded together and to one conductor of the feedline.  

I liked playing with the things like the thermocouple you made and I looked up some things.  Thanks man.
